rationale: In the theory section, the authors explicitly cite Zhu and Meyers-Levy (2005) when introducing their own two-way distinction about what may spill over from art, which is more than a passing literature-list mention. They cite it again in the discussion to align music's referential versus embodied meanings with their own content-dependent versus content-independent account, showing clear conceptual use. The citing paper does not build on the cited paper's specific processing-intensity boundary conditions, so the citation functions mainly as transfer of an idea across domains.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Zhu and Meyers-Levy's meaning distinction in music as a conceptual template and applies it to visual art. This borrowed distinction helps frame hypotheses about whether art effects come from image content or from content-independent luxury connotations.
